interview_rounds.did_well: Incredible craft. Presentation was gorgeous and the content even better. Great taste. Photography is excellent. Solid storyteller. Ramped Ramp team from 1 to 20. Ramp focused on speed of execution. Rebranded Ramp out in the open. Developed beautiful photo library with Midjourney, combined with great customer photography done by a photographer. Getting inspired by where the customers would be (Financial times, monocle, etc). Managed an agency to scale the visual system in a beautiful way. Very creative work ('Doing finance' campaign was awesome, and hitting a tone that really matches Plaid's).
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Not as much experience in concepting/advertising. Knows who to bring for help, but clearly has a huge background in design/systems. Both case studies were very similar (though Ramp's went a little wider). Both demonstrated Aaron's incredible taste and design skills. Would have wanted to see more breadth of work. Still early in leadership journey of scaling up and delegating/empowering. Works a lot with agencies, but we didn't hear much about the in-house team(s).

interview_rounds.did_well: Strong experience with photo shoots, events, etc.

name: Adam Kennedy
debrief_notes: Seems solid (at least based off the 30 min chat we had). Would love to see their portfolio review (if we do one). Would make a great partner to Product Design.

interview_rounds.did_well: Solid and relevant experience of working with Product Design in different settings, including at fintech startups. Good breadth of solid creative work, with lots of web and campaigns work (not just a ton of video advertising work). Thoughtful and impact oriented. Approachable, easy to talk to. Seem methodical in their approach to up-leveling the work of a team - start with building a common lexicon/aligned understanding of what great looks like with stakeholders, so it's easier to rally the team around that shared path. More clarity + helps 'shorten the distance to great design'.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Didn't always give concrete examples, though we only had 30 min so it was really hard to be specific without sacrificing time.

interview_rounds.did_well: Creative. Think outside the box and love 0-1 work. Good experience rethinking experiences from the ground up in the Concept lab team at Amazon. Craft isn't super sharp/pixel-perfect, but still elegant and modern, with some decent prototyping. In touch with industry and new technology. Friendly and solid presenter.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Scope of what they've worked on doesn't seem very large. Not a ton of experience working in a larger design initiative and leading. At Dapper, they were in a team of 2. At Amazon, they were the only designer in their org.
interview_rounds.thoughts: I'm a low 3. I wasn't blown away.

interview_rounds.did_well: Strong background in writing. Spotify Year in Music - a brand campaign turned into one of the biggest assets/impact for the business. Thoughtful - clear POV on positioning as the challenger brand against big tech with punk/edgy vibe and humor. Prove it by showing it: philosophy leans towards giving ideas legs to run and evaluate performance, versus over-thinking/over-planning. Great ideas on how to scale the impact (e.g. make a wrapped for artists to leverage the echo they can generate). Overcame the challenge of staying fresh/edgy relevant when doing the same campaign every year. Clearly can lead a creative team to incredible heights. Spotify as a brand has done incredibly well under his leadership.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Storytelling can be a little too mellow, and long-winded. At Spotify, there was a high level of trust into Alex and his team - would Alex be able to acquire the same level of trust at Plaid? Alex has worked the team up to 70+, and with huge budgets and large marketing org. Can he get back to scrappy/small things? Didn't seem to have a super clear approach to how to scale himself while staying close to the work that matters. Didn't come off as the most organized/best planner. Anytime he was asked about timelines or lead times, it sounded like he was more going with the flow than really being thoughtful/proactive about it.

interview_rounds.did_well: Good experience in Fintech. Humble and Curious. Good presenter, passionate and clear. Overall pretty solid execution skills. Nothing outstanding, but solid across the board.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Light product design experience (6-7y). Promoted to Staff just last year. Still working on a single project, and managing a junior designer. Limited mentorship experience. Have been mentoring a couple of junior designers the last few months, but no one senior. Very little leadership exposure/experience. Have mostly been in pretty junior position until recently, and even now are really only working with 1 senior PM.

interview_rounds.did_well: Can wear a lot of hats. Brings creative direction, filming and editing together. Charismatic presenter - friendly, clear, engaging. Work for Airbnb was beautifully executed - followed the high level goals and creative direction all throughout, from cinematography and filming with different lenses to the editing. Seems to have a clear POV on how to work in an organized, scalable way (e.g. this is how we set the lights, this is how we organize the shoot, etc). Capable of adapting to needs, budgets, resources, timing and focused on delivering quality.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Not clear how strongly Alfredo feels about his editing skills.

interview_rounds.did_well: Started their presentation by talking about their approach, which is anchored into user needs, JTBD, etc. Collaborating closely with Product Design and UX research. 'Error messages have a special place in my heart' signaled their attention to details and care for user needs and fulfilling them. Clear and articulate, including when answering questions. C's shared work that directly impacted the design of the end product (example of Accounts & profiles entry point, or the Airtable app creation flow). Experience working with disciplines like legal/privacy at Meta (which has a pretty robust process) - and on how to pushback/find the right tradeoff. Thoughtful, passionate, user-centric. Opinionated and honest about their direct contributions and where they felt it fell short of their aspirations, but pragmatic and able to make tradeoffs. Experience being first designer at Airtable, but also being part of a bigger content discipline at Meta or Netflix.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Communication style during presentation felt a bit robotic. Candidate was reading a script, which helped them be succinct and clear, but not super engaging.

interview_rounds.did_well: Very user-centric and impact-oriented in their work. Smart, passionate and opinionated, articulate, collaborative and pragmatic. Humble about their shortcomings and honest about when their POV turned out to be the wrong one. Experience working on very high-touch consumer-facing interfaces, and complex technical flows, and passionate about 'Making complicated things simple'.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Shared the story on how they felt mis-leveled when they joined Meta, and seemed to have fixated on it, asking how they were tracking against a promo to their manager at every 1:1, which showed focus and ambition, but also a lack of maturity and trust.

name: Andrea Balthazar
recommendation: 2 - No Hire (interview focuses), 2 - No Hire (portfolio), 2 - No Hire (principles, shadowing Rubi Santoro)
debrief_notes: Interview Focuses: I didn't get a ton of signal in this interview (C tended to ramble a bit) but what I got didn't make me feel confident they'd succeed in this role. Nothing was terrible, but very little stood out as a key strength. Portfolio: Though this candidate has clear experience and strengths, I didn't see enough depth in how they lead, in the scope of projects they can support, in the impact they can have, and how they can raise the quality bar on a team.

interview_rounds.did_well: Solid presenter. Case study grounded in real people problem, with insights. Clear impact of the work. Candidate was close to the work (required, with jr designers). Exp navigating different org structures and cultures. Exp running Growth-oriented teams, experimenting, etc.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Fairly junior team / how effective would they be with a more senior team? Case study they spent most of their time on was very small in scope. We didn't see any divergence, little iteration, etc. No strong examples of C advocating for something. Work was overall looking solid, but was all using the Base UI system, and there's no evidence the C challenged their team to push beyond it (incl. in sprint). We didn't hear much about XFN partners.

interview_rounds.did_well: Great background and set of experiences, both at similarly-sized companies, and bigger ones. Great craft and craft leadership. Pragmatic and impact-oriented, but able to push teams to flex their muscles to ship work they're proud of, and have them work within an existing system, and/or pushing the system forward. Clear, transparent, candid, creative and proactive. Great mix of projects shown / one smaller in time/scope but with top-down mandate and great urgency, another much more ambitious and with larger impact, with a bottoms-up approach and much more organizational hurdles to overcome. Seem really flexible and adaptable to their environment. Able to navigate orgs and complexity well, and deliver great outcomes.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: We could have seen more explorations/work that didn't make the cut. Though the presentation was packed with content, and it's clear that the work happened.

interview_rounds.did_well: Strong systems thinking background. Experienced in scaling design teams and processes. Has managed bigger teams, clear approach to vision setting, growth, conflicts, momentum, quality, etc. Deep experience in leading visual systems, craft, etc / perfect for this role. Good cultural alignment with Plaid's values. Clear communication style. Demonstrated success in cross-functional leadership.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Sometimes could have given me more concrete examples, but I didn't fish for them too much, and when I did the answers were always solid. Experience seems very systems/visual design focused - they'll likely have to lean onto their senior designers and xfn partners for product strategy. Haven't had much chance to contribute meaningfully to how the design org operates at their most recent companies (Meta, Coinbase) but have done things like rewriting the career ladder/growth guide, etc.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Great leader, who will bring new ideas and perspective, all while being flexible and open-minded enough to adapt to Plaid and fit right in.

interview_rounds.did_well: Experience at different tech companies (Google, Dropbox, Opendoor, Walmart) and being the sole DPM (which forced him to consider everything). Talked about culture, creating momentum, building relationships with designers. Thinks about the end to end journey and creates tools and processes to support projects throughout. Turned the creative team from reactive to proactive, being more on top of asks and prioritizing better. Love that he conducted a study on how much time designers were spending in meetings/reviews vs designing.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Shared a mini case study about a photo studio at Hey Day, which went so quick it was more confusing than anything. Timeline for his needs assessment seemed large - can he work as fast as we work at Plaid? Not the best constructed presentation (though things were pretty clear).
interview_rounds.thoughts: Andrew has worked with different design teams, at different companies, which much have exposed him to different challenges and needs. John vouching for him is also a plus. That said, I'm not confident Andrew can adapt to our fast paced, high expectation environment, and follow in Ellis' footsteps versus try to reinvent the wheel, nor do I think he brings something unique that'll make him successful in the long run.

interview_rounds.did_well: Fairly solid experience working with Design, at CashApp and their own startup Qvivr. Shared a good example of a consumer vision they established at CashApp for Families, the evolution of the Teens Card team they were on. Their process was rooted in team brainstorms and research insights, then validated by Research, before they started building the product and learning from the data. Good POV on the role of Product Management in relations to other disciplines. e.g. 'A PM is not a mini-CEO'. Sees Data science as the fourth leg of the stool.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Some aspects of their worldview seem at odds with our culture. The point of view they seem to have for the role Design plays seem more anchored in an early 2000s era when it was more about visual/graphic design than in the present, where it's about bringing clarity from ambiguous customer needs and internal requirements, the user journey and end-to-end experience. At no point did they talk about customer needs, nor did they show some care for getting the details right. They didn't display a lot of care for what they've built.

interview_rounds.did_well: Interesting Product + Engineering background. Some experience building consumer-facing experiences at Facebook. Fairly solid experience working with Design, at times as a manager, and otherwise as a peer (in particular at Venmo and Facebook). Good principles on shipping high-quality products, though it's not clear they've actually applied those principles in their work (they only shared situations when they didn't apply them).
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Actual product management experience felt a bit minimal - 1.5 years at FB, and managing a couple of PMs at Attune. Shared an example of their ability to build compelling consumer vision from their work at Facebook on Locations. The process to get there seemed fairly clear, and anchored in Research and data. It felt pretty bottoms-up, but at no moment did the Candidate talk about executive input and buy-in, how they communicated that vision, etc. They jumped straight to their experimentation plan, etc - it didn't feel like something they've done multiple times and have a winning recipe for. Minor thing on style: Our conversation felt awkward at times, as the candidate took long pauses after each question, then followed by speaking incredibly fast.

interview_rounds.did_well: Experience with end-to-end brand campaigns at Zendesk. Experience with video production. Thoughtful approach to communication: asking execs for their preference.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Storytelling was a bit all over the place - hard to follow at times. Struggling with being concise - answers were long-winded and at times hard to follow - worried that it might affect Beryl's ability to communicate effectively with key stakeholders. Experience limited to large (5) producing team, where role is mostly limited to particular campaigns. How much will Beryl be able to impact beyond project management and help Ellis across the org?

name: Bethany Valoe
debrief_notes: Portfolio: I'm a high 3 – there was lots to like, especially in the first case study.

interview_rounds.did_well: Solid experience, with mix of agency and client work. Very relevant experience in Fintech (Affirm, 5y). Currently leading a small team of brand designers. Clear presenter / easy to follow presentation (though lacked a bit of energy). Very clear process. Solid visual work, seem crafty and willing to play with AI tools to bring their ideas to life. Business challenge clearly laid out, audience insights, etc + success metrics. Navigated a complex/high stake campaign for Affirm/Apple Pay. Design for Affirm on Apple Pay was scalable to 100s of variations. Created animated digital display ads, email campaigns, etc. Shared success metrics, which looked really solid. Did entire Affirm campaign with just their copywriter, and worked directly with photographer and videographer. Seem really willing to try new tools (Figma Buzz, AI for storyboarding, etc). Execution on gradients for Lululemon summer campaign was solid.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Presentation lacked energy, despite the content being really cool. We only saw a bit of breadth of explorations for the high level concept of the Affirm campaign. I wish we had seen more things that didn't make the cut. I was craving to hear more about how they collaborate with other designers, align with the brand guidelines, etc.
interview_rounds.thoughts: I'm a high 3 - there was lots to like, especially in the first case study.

interview_rounds.did_well: Thoughtful, intentional and pragmatic about their process and work. Act as an owner - willing to roll up their sleeves and do everything to make something successful, best exemplified by their work on the Affirm/Apple Pay campaign where they did everything from concept/design to pre-prod work, animation, production design, sound design, etc. Cares about craft, consider themselves a minimalist. Also passionate about photography. Heavy user of AI, as a creative thought partner, for image gen or sound design. Also solid in editing/motion. Great mentorship experience, and aspiring to continue mentoring people while focusing more on their own execution than in their current role.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Nothing stood out.

interview_rounds.did_well: Lot of experience designing complex tools - at AWS, Salesforce, Flexport and now Stripe. Strong knowledge of payments/fraud space, having worked on Signal-equivalent at Stripe. Can collaborate closely with ML engineers, data scientists, etc and come up with some great products. Rock solid execution - with a very strong spike in data visualization, and creating simple and useful interfaces that achieve complex tasks. Solid 'content creator' - creates deck or pages to articulate his thoughts, ideas, concepts, processes... internally. Some management experience - managed a team of 9 at Flexport at some point. Seems to have a solid business acumen.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: No consumer-facing experience. Has been designing complex tools for last 10 years. Presentation style is okay, not amazing.
interview_rounds.thoughts: I'd like to get a second opinion on Brad. What I've seen is really solid - but since it was more of a polished presentation, I didn't get to ask them a lot of the questions I'd want to ask. My open questions: Is the spike they have in designing complex tools transferrable to consumer-facing work? Can they shine on Link? How do they lead other designers? Can they zoom in/out of projects quickly and provide value? Can they handle a large plate of small things just as well as they do a large initiative?

name: Brady Voss
debrief_notes: Pre-screen: I've personally worked with Brady for a few years at Facebook. Brady is extremely talented, approachable and engaging, and has many years of experience shipping large-scale products with a high level of polish. Fantastic consumer-facing track-record, great craft, solid leadership experience, management and mentorship experience, great collaborator. Only open question is how well they will adapt to a fast-pace, scrappy environment like Plaid. Portfolio: Very disappointed in Brady's portfolio presentation. Presentation wasn't engaging, most slides were too text-heavy, content was focused on the wrong aspects of the work. This presentation highlighted that Brady is well-tuned for communicating within a huge organization but may not be able to adapt their framing to a more focused audience. Based on this presentation, I'm not confident Brady can apply his experience and skillset to Plaid and be successful.

interview_rounds.did_well: Fantastic consumer-facing track-record, shipping high quality experiences on high scale products at Microsoft (Windows phone, Xbox), Facebook (Videos), and Netflix. Great craft - Solid product thinker, with excellent interaction & visual design chops, and solid prototyper. Solid leadership experience, influencing executives and key stakeholders, seeing around the corner and pushing their vision to the roadmap, leading other designers along the way. Management and mentorship experience. Great collaborator - approachable, great thought partner, who will push others to do better work in an inspiring and actionable way.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Nothing standing out from this interview.

name: Brittney R. Villalva
debrief_notes: What did they do well on?: Love that they're comfortable using Figma variable for controlling/maintaining strings. Appreciated the little work she had done on the consent screen. Shows real interest. Clearly using AI to research problems, create first drafts. Thinks about systems and user expectations/needs – overall solid product muscle. What did they do poorly on?: Only one case study – seems like a big miss. Would have wanted to see a lot more. Didn't talk a lot about design partners, how they collaborate. Didn't get into the details on the why behind certain words. Would have wanted a deeper dive into their craft rather than just a high level overview.

interview_rounds.did_well: Love that they're comfortable using Figma variable for controlling/maintaining strings. I appreciated the little work she had done on the consent screen. Shows real interest. Clearly using AI to research problems, create first drafts, etc. Reasons like a product designer.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Didn't talk a lot about design partners, how they collaborate, etc. Only one case study. Didn't get into the details a lot on the why behind certain words. I would have wanted a deeper dive into their craft rather than just a high level overview of the project. We didn't see a lot of work-in-progress/explorations.

interview_rounds.did_well: Experience at a B2B company. Work was well presented. Able to wear many hats and be scrappy. Problem solver. Friendly personality. Seems easy to work with and humble. Excellent work. Both projects had great outcomes, and the work was really well produced. Passionate, yet pragmatic. Clearly had a list of things he'd fix if he could go back and redo the pieces, but also pragmatic that no shoot is ever perfect and satisfied with the final output. Organized - video workflow is clear and structured.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Only part I didn't have a lot of signal on was how comfortable he is in working fully solo (vs relying on contractors to help).

interview_rounds.did_well: Incredible experience. Worked at Apple during the golden age of brand design (Steve Jobs era). Helped scale the first internal creative team at Facebook/Meta from 12 to 150 while fine-tuning their advertising and design skills. Volunteered to build a creative team to support product marketing, which grew from 3 to 100 people, developing expertise in organizational design and creative operations. Re-orged the creative teams at Facebook, forming Creative X, which grew to 250 people and supported the family of apps. Led brand at Circle through the IPO, which has been a great success. Passionate about the new technology waves and the opportunity they give to brand, marketing, and helping those technologies speak to their audience. Aims to work with world-class leaders and contribute to the evolution of design, branding, marketing, and communication.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Looking for an environment to build best-in-class products in AI. Need to play that angle with him. Driven by growth and the desire to be at the cutting edge, not just part of the fray. Currently located in Costa Rica, but moving back to the bay in 6 months. Has a house in Half-Moon Bay and kids are already interviewing with schools here.

interview_rounds.did_well: Very clear and engaging presenter. Even when walking me through an 'unstructured' Figma file, they were able to efficiently and clearly cover a pretty large initiative, and answer most of my follow-up questions. Thoughtful, principled and organized - As Cara walked me through an important initiative at Ancestry (redesigning their core navigation/interface), they highlighted how they approached the project, identified some of the organizational challenges, handled a company-wide pivot, built momentum to get engineering funding, leveraged prototyping and UXR to challenge their hypotheses, clearly listed out their hypotheses to track experiments in a structured way, etc. Recognized early in the process that the project and the team didn't have any success metrics, and only some KPIs + made some recommendations the team is now following. On a product with an older audience that's change-averse, Cara managed to push through some important UI redesigns. Clear and effective approach to leadership/stakeholder management - building credibility and trust, thinking ahead of what each audience member might be looking for/thinking about (and answering those questions in the presentation to build that credibility), exec summary, etc. Know how to operate in fast-paced environment efficiently: leveraging async communication, sharing loom videos walking people through their work and ideas, etc.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Didn't mention how their work lined up to the broader company goals (though they did mention the company pivot and how it impacted the project). Not a red flag given the focus of this particular project - but worth exploring their business acumen in future chats. They mentioned enjoying making prototypes to test their ideas, though I didn't see any during our chat. I wonder how well they leverage this tool.

interview_rounds.did_well: Work was well grounded in data, UXR results and principles. Case studies were pretty easy to follow, very focused on the work and the explorations, went deep into the details, and work/decisions were well articulated. Candidate has dealt with complex problems, in very different surfaces and for different audiences (B2B, B2C, Ads, Consumer AI, etc) and did a great job describing the complex dynamics between Product, Legal and Marketing goals. Experience crafting content guidelines/principles or refining them. Mature thinking when it comes to working cross-functionally, working with data, and crafting work for scale. Experience working on 0-1 products like Gemini at Google show an ability to work in a more 'startup' environment and working fast.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Case studies focused a lot on the work and the iterations (which was great) but at times lacked some color on how the C dealt with cross-functional partners and their needs.
interview_rounds.thoughts: A lot of positive signal coming from this presentation - Carston has a solid set of experiences and demonstrated his ability to work on very complex problems and iterating his way to the right solution, leveraging UXR, data, competitive analysis, or intuition.

interview_rounds.did_well: Solid experience, working both on B2B and B2C, and in fast-moving environments like Google Gemini. Seem to be comfortable managing complexity, wrangling lots of different stakeholders, and knowing which battles to fight and which to let go of. Knows how to prioritize and where to apply their attention and go deep, and how to scale their support to lower priority projects/teams with AI+office hours. Able to work with little to no direction and deal with ambiguity. Shared the story of the transition from Bard to Gemini and their role in pushing the product in a new direction. Good system thinking and experience building a scalable content design discipline on a team that lacks one (e.g. Google Payments platform).
interview_rounds.did_not_well: I didn't get a ton of signal on how they act as a bar raiser on a team, how they collaborate with designers, legal, etc. I'll be looking for signal on those in the portfolio preso.

name: Cassandra Lim
debrief_notes: Principles: This is a soft no, because I don't think this candidate is quite ready for the work we have in the pipeline for them. They'd be most effective in a small product team, owning a fairly well understood problem space, but still have to grow their ability to collaborate with other designers on bigger challenges. Portfolio: I'm a very soft 3. No bad signal, but also not blown away by anything. This candidate seems early in their career, and more of a D4 with solid potential than an established D5.

interview_rounds.did_well: Very clear presentation structure, articulating the level of depth they'd go into in each project in their intro, then clearly framing the problem, audience, etc. Solid presenter, who's clearly deep into their domain, yet could clearly articulate the problem space and user needs. Customer-focused - Case study grounded in real customer pain points, identified by research, and clearly has a deep understanding of their domain. Polished Figma file and work. Good articulation of their design decisions and iterations (although some of them were questionable). Experience in working on a complex problem space - Condition design for the compensation policy tool seemed very intuitive and powerful. Did a good job articulating why they were excited about Plaid and their strengths as a designer.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Seems early in their product thinking journey - thinking at the feature level, not as much at the product or system-level, and the articulation of certain design decisions they made seemed to have a lot of holes. Low signal on their ability to collaborate with other designers, how they think about contributing back to the system, etc.

interview_rounds.did_well: Really proud of their impact on the Performance management product, because it had a real business need (Lattice was coming out with their own HR solution, which meant Rippling product offering was going to look incomplete in comparison) and filling a real customer need. It was 0 to 1 and existential for the company, and they got to work end-to-end, including with product marketing/brand on the launch. Have an appetite for personal growth and are ambitious - they mentioned having flagged to their manager early on at Rippling that they wanted to do more than work on the small features they were assigned to, and wanted to show they could be a senior product designer. They were put in charge of the Perf management project and they did really well and got promoted within 6 months. They built a fairly strong understanding of their user needs, and used it to advocate for certain features or solutions. They mentioned having had multiple reviews with the CEO, whom they had to convince was pushing for a POV that wasn't shared by customers in research. Clearly excited about Plaid - they mentioned that they had applied in 2020 already (but were only 1 year into their career at the time).
interview_rounds.did_not_well: This candidate seems still very early in their journey. Their self-awareness is still developing, they're not yet at a stage where they can help raise the bar on a team. They're still sharpening up their own process and don't seem quite able to adapt easily to ever-changing environments (they mentioned the struggle they had when their project team was given a hard deadline and had to operate with more scrappiness and urgency).

name: Charlene Tsang
debrief_notes: Principles: I'm not confident Charlene would raise the bar on our team. Craft is ok, but not impressive. No technical skills or motion design skills. Portfolio (note: feedback was submitted in the wrong form): During our chat, the candidate never talked about business goals or audience needs. A lot of the answers felt very shallow. Didn't display a great ownership mentality. Overall underwhelmed by this candidate, who seemed promising on paper but whose answers felt very surface-level and uninspiring.

interview_rounds.did_well: Good breadth and depth of experience. Solid system thinking, building reusable components, giving wireframing tools for partners to use, etc. Program manage most of their projects. A little bit of people management experience. Mature in their thinking, seem to be able to navigate complexity and partner with product.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Craft is ok, but not impressive. I don't know if they'd be able to express our brand in new ways on web, and design experiences that push us forward. No technical skills or motion design skills. Storytelling wasn't super strong / it felt a little chaotic in structure at times. Outside of 1 example where they talked about a specific persona and how a particular design solution was addressing the needs of that persona, their decision process seemed more based on 'what will engineering be able to do' than 'what's best solving our problem'.

interview_rounds.did_well: Candidate was pretty self aware. They mentioned what skills they want to grow (learn HTML/CSS, motion design). Able to project manage, prioritize, and work with engineering on picking the most feasible solution. Managed a small design team in a Player/Coach capacity. Built some wireframe components so copywriters can explore the content of a page without having to wait for a first design.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: During our chat, the candidate never talked about business goals or audience needs. A lot of the answers felt very shallow and it was hard to get good signal - as an example, to the question 'what is a goal you set for yourself (not assigned by someone else) and how did you go about achieving it?', their answer was 'learning new skills. I love to learn new skills'. Didn't display a great ownership mentality: often blamed engineering capabilities as the reason for tradeoffs, or the quality of the final output. Didn't exemplify a strong Championship team mentality - when asked what's an example of critical feedback they've given to someone, they shared: 'When I was a manager, I provided performance reviews and advised a visual-based designer to consider more UI/UX aspects and look at resources like Pinterest for inspiration'.

interview_rounds.did_well: Tons of varied experience. Passionate about hands-on production design vs leadership roles. Values calculated risk-taking in design. Cares deeply about quality of their work, and likes to move fast. Recently learned Banner Flow for motion design as a self-initiated goal to expand skillset. Excited about learning FinTech industry (completely new sector). Appreciates Plaid's collaborative culture and thoughtful approach to growth. Values the team's openness to creative risk-taking. Demonstrates willingness to push beyond comfort zone (e.g., motion design). Interested in exploring AI tools and adapting to future production design needs.

Work is well polished, prototypes were robust and elegant. Presentation was clear, well put together, and they were very clear and crisp in their delivery. Design decisions were clearly articulated and grounded in project goals and user insights. Design process is clear, iterative, with good breadth of explorations. Vision was ambitious, but C were also able to gracefully break it apart to get its essence/core value shipped out in an elegant way. Experience designing reusable components. Candidate was honest about how projects actually went down, sharing when things got descoped or when experiments didn't perform well. Solid set of experiences at Meta, Coinbase, and Square. Experience collaborating on projects across multiple teams, on ambiguous projects as well as growth-oriented experiments. Strong care for craft. Thoughtful, intentional in their work. Clear, concise communicator. Really easy to talk to, and answers were always full of concrete examples. Self-aware and honest - was very open with their shortcomings, growth areas, etc. Pragmatic, able to move fast but also cares about details and getting things right. Work is grounded in user insights and goals, and not just aspirational.

interview_rounds.did_not_well: Concerned about craft. Some misalignment on deck make me feel like when they aren't operating within a set design system, may struggle a lot. Spikes in process - arguably.

name: Chris Sanchez
debrief_notes: I don't think Chris is a strong enough candidate for this role.

interview_rounds.did_well: Very strong consumer-facing experience (Uber, Skillz, TikTok). Visual and motion design work is very solid, especially their work at Uber. At Uber, they were reporting into the Principal product designer, and have had first-hand exposure to what that role entails. Excellent at producing assets/presentations for internal/external purposes (background in marketing/brand).
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Presentation skills are average. Not super engaging of a speaker. Experience at Skillz as 'Principal' was really just them and 1 jr designer. Unsure of how strong their product skills really are. No signal on their leadership experience.

name: Christie Freeman
debrief_notes: Portfolio: I'm a high 3 – the work and experience feel very solid and would warrant a 4, but the signal I got around humility and collaboration makes me doubt a little bit. I'll try to dive into those in my 1:1 if I can. Principles: Christie is a rock-solid content designer, with a ton of experience, is hard-working, very passionate and driven, curious and opinionated. They'll bring a ton of energy and will be an active voice on the team. It'll be on us to make sure we set them up for success and help channel that energy the right way, but I'm confident they'll have a lot of impact at Plaid.

interview_rounds.did_well: Impressive set of experiences and past work, and clear capacity to operate in different environments and different industries with different audiences. Impact beyond content - Have led their own research, an accessibility effort at Rivian, onboarding global team members. Were successful at Rivian as first content designer and grew a discipline around them. Good storyteller - their articulation of how they explored the windshield wipers language at Rivian was engaging and clear. Approach their work with curiosity and asking questions, pushing their thinking beyond the obvious. Seems collaborative and a solid problem solver, illustrated by the example of the naming group they put together at Rivian.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Maybe a bit of ego in how they talk about themselves and their work and how proud they are of it. There was a lot of 'I'm very proud, this is beautiful language, we did a great job...', or the fact that they wouldn't do anything differently on their work at Rivian. Seem to be very opinionated, but unsure if those opinions are loosely held. Seem comfortable working with designers, but it wasn't clear how they work with other XFN partners. Their responses to questions were a little evasive and didn't give us a lot of clear signal. Lack of attention to detail (typo on the first sentence of their online portfolio, showing 'Presentation-for-Asana' in their figma file).

name: Christine Nedilsky
debrief_notes: I wish we had seen a little more in this presentation – All of my signals feel a little weak, but the little I got was all positive.

interview_rounds.did_well: Solid presentation skills - good articulation of their problems, goals, success criteria, etc. Clear intentionality - C had a clear POV and shared the why behind many of their design decisions. Detail-oriented work. Both projects presented were high stakes, high complexity and with lots of stakeholders (legal, privacy, etc) to wrangle.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: I wish we had seen a little more depth in the work: process, explorations, etc. It wasn't clear how the C led finding alignment with other functions vs taking a backseat.

interview_rounds.did_well: Very creative thinker. Work they've done for Turbotax in particular is quite impressive. Principled, thoughtful, risk-taking (in their creative work). Good POV on how to up-level the work from a team.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Started off, in their intro, by telling me how negatively they felt about having to help product work at Intuit, which they felt was not what they're good at, and that it was their motivation for applying to this role, which I found... a bit unsettling? With the exception of some of their work at Intuit, they seem to have really focused their creative work in the past to video content, TV advertising, etc. Their work at Intuit is much closer to Product, but it's really just the last couple of years. Overall, they did not give me confidence they'd be a great partner in nurturing a strong relationship between product and brand.
interview_rounds.thoughts: A bit of a strange interview - they started off as trying to interview me - I was able to steer the convo into asking my own questions and giving them the time at the end to ask me what they wanted to know, though.

interview_rounds.did_well: Great craft. Cares a lot and sweats the details. Good presenter (though at times a bit too quick). Clear, concise, good framing/context-setting, including what skills they wanted to highlight in each case study. Pretty solid set of experiences. Solid product thinking - good articulation of their solutions and decisions, grounded in user experience, customer insights, and data. Impact oriented. Clearly very excited about the impact this feature may have had on helping close a new deal (and also pragmatic about the relative importance of that feature in the whole deal). Solid breadth of explorations. Explores ambitious solutions, but also pragmatic and finds ways to incorporate ideas from their north star into the MVP.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Not a lot of prototyping.

interview_rounds.did_well: Thoughtful. C had good reflections on how they've grown over the years and what they've learned from mistakes or successes. Act as an owner + Solid product sense - C talked about a project at Asana they advocated for - new user onboarding for the Inbox, and articulated why it could be so impactful for the business (accelerating a flywheel effect that would drive a lot of engagement). They found believers in the company, rallied them around the idea and then shopped it to the right people to get it funded. Sweats the details - hardworking and passionate, C started as a UI/visual designer and cares deeply about craft. They've since expanded to the full Product Design skillset, but are still deeply engaged in crafting amazing experience. Ambitious - both with their design and their personal growth. Main reason they mentioned being on the lookout for a new opportunity is they find themselves not supported in their personal growth. Like to work transparently, share their work regularly and often, and saw their eyes light up when I mention how we use tools like Campsite to share our work.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Nothing stood out in this interview. They did really solidly.

interview_rounds.did_well: Strong brand design bg. Has done a lot of great work at Rippling, pushing their visual style to new heights, and leading really fun campaigns, including some clever OOH. At Uber, also led the brand design function when it scaled down to 5 during Covid-era. Did some significant brand evolution there to bring Uber and Uber Eats closer. Has led a 20-ish person team (incl contractors) at Rippling. Great craft and attention to details. Currently diving deeper into video. Loves leveraging AI in brand. Seems really flexible, able to get into the weeds of the work where it's helpful, but also stay in the review seat as needed. Thinks a lot about emotional connection, making work that resonates deeply with the person seeing it. Gave great examples with OOH work for Rippling. Experience branding a B2B Saas company. That's quite relevant!
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Not a lot of signal on his copywriting/storytelling skills (though he seems to be a good storyteller himself). Leans more brand and digital than experiential/events (not a bad thing, just a flag).
interview_rounds.thoughts: Not as senior as some of the candidates we've talked to. Probably more of an M7 caliber with really good energy and a strong design sense. This role seems like a perfect next step for him in his career, if we're willing to make a bet. I foresee an easy ramp up and a clear growth path.

interview_rounds.did_well: Very clear storyteller, easy to follow his process. Excellent craft - work looked great. The rippling booth work was really solid - very noticeable, memorable, exciting, and overall well put together. I appreciated the presentation format, with some highlight reel + high level summary of his approach/process, followed by the 2 case studies with a clear angle to each.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Booth work was great at grabbing people's attention, but not immediately clear how it connected to Rippling's offering. Both projects were agency-first. Wish we had seen more in-house work. It wasn't always very clear what Dan's leadership impact was on the projects. I'm not sure Dan hit his own goal with the second case study, which was meant to highlight cross-functional wrangling/alignment. He stayed pretty surface-level on it IMO, and it didn't give me a clear picture of how he handles exec stakeholders, especially when they're passionate about the brand. Comments on pushing the team when 'they're not burnt out' to put in the hours didn't feel quite aligned with how we work on Design at Plaid.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Solid craft, solid storyteller, good breadth of work / but overall, more of an M7 with a clear design spike than an M8.

interview_rounds.did_well: Took on an ambitious program at Athleta to switch the creative team from Adobe/Keynote to Figma. Figured out a process to get the team ramped up and transitioned while minimizing impact on roadmap.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: First case study felt shallow. At times, it felt like Dana was telling us about the features Figma offers more than she talked about the work she had done to make it successful at Athleta.

interview_rounds.did_well: Good ownership mentality. Seems to be ready to do what it takes to make something work. Experience creating guidelines and working end-to-end on campaigns across multiple channels. Seems like a solid collaborator.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: Didn't share many explorations/things that didn't work. Hard to know how widely they explored. Experience seems focused on performance marketing and small campaigns. After 4 years at Yelp, I would have imagined they'd have had a bigger project/case study to show us than the Services.

name: Dominic Salbatera
debrief_notes: Portfolio: Too many holes in this candidate's skillset. Their storytelling and product thinking are solid, but the rest of the skillset we need simply isn't there.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Very clear presentation of the problem, context, setup
- Solid understanding of their product and audience
- Great storyteller. Good energy, clear, engaging
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Poor visual design.
- Clear lack of divergent thinking. Jumping to solution quickly and we didn't see any explorations/variations
- Interaction design. Very questionable design decisions (a table with 54k results and showing 10 results per page??). Things were all over the place, lacked structure.
interview_rounds.thoughts: AEXP case study notes: 2 week project to design + 4-6 weeks to build. Rough visual design, no explorations, questionable interaction choices. Good storyteller, seemed to have good answers to questions. Experience at small software companies isn't always transferrable. Worry they might be a great storyteller hiding big holes in design skills. Solo-designer.

Discussed AEXP project, goal setting around leveraging data over qualitative research, property explorer redesign, portfolio monitor project, collaboration with designers. Mentioned wanting to hone visual craft, real time collaboration, continue leveraging product data. Positive view of Plaid from personal experience.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Good presenter. Friendly tone, easy to listen to and follow, articulate.
- Solid product thinking. For each case study, their framing of the problem, and their assessment of what works/doesn't work with the current version of the product, was solid.
- Customer-centric work / ran a card-sorting exercise and other qual research methods, gathered customer feedback, and did a competitive analysis before jumping into the solution space.
- Solid design process
- Solid generative blue-sky thinking, that informed the final designs
- Experience having to balance Legal constraints and customer needs/building the best user experience
- Execution seems polished (within the systems used by Guideline at least)
- Humble in where they fell short (trying to do too much). Good reflections
- Solid experience in working with Legal and good thoughts on how to have a productive partnership
interview_rounds.did_not_well: - Not always clear where their direct contributions ended and that of other designers started
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: PM @ Growth Credit (2y), Designer @ Enzo Wealth (3y), Contract work at Citizen Health and Plaid.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Displayed humility and honesty, both when owning up mistakes they've made and what they've learned, and in sharing what skills they still need to grow
- Seem to care a lot about craft and building great products
- Are very disciplined and care about delivering products that reach their goals. Will work tirelessly (and after hours) to get things done (but struggle to delegate/ask for help)
- Seem able to move fast when needed, but also to follow a rigorous process and be thoughtful when they can.
- Good collaborator - PMs ask to work with Dustin over other designers
- Solid self-awareness. Shared 4 skills they want to get better at (when asked for 1-2)
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Product thinking seems to be an area of growth. They self-identified that they often struggle to have a clear opinion/point of view and to communicate it well, which has, at times, backfired. This may be a challenge.
- Still new to working at bigger companies and will likely some time to really operate autonomously. Few mentions of impact when talking about their work.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Discussed Guideline Pro project, presentation skills growth, project failure at Qualcast with CTO, conflict resolution with EM. Top strengths: visual design, collaboration. Growth areas: delegating work, having strong opinions, XFN collaboration, conflict resolution.

name: Elliott Htchison
debrief_notes: Portfolio: Craft looks solid (after we got a chance to see some explorations). Transparent and humble. Once we asked for explorations, we saw really cool work. But not a very engaging presenter. Presentation lacked content. First case study was 15 min long, second was 5 min long. Felt like the candidate really hadn't prepared for it. Overall nothing that gets me excited enough to compensate for what feels like lack of preparation/care for the interview.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Work looks solid (after we got a chance to see some explorations)
- Figma prototyping for storyboarding motion
- Experience building figma components
- Transparent and humble
- Once we asked for explorations, we saw really cool work. I wish the candidate had oriented their presentation differently so we could see more of it.
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Not a lot of agency on the content/messaging/structure of the pages. Mostly working out of copy written by PMMs
- Not a very engaging presenter.
- Presentation lacked content. First case study was 15 min long, second was 5 min long. Felt like the candidate really hadn't prepared for it.

interview_rounds.thoughts: Discussed hardest project (client with strong POV), inspiration from boss at Tiled, getting better at Figma (seems new to it), feedback about showing ideas that may not make it. Shared real estate in NYC project as most successful. Took current job to be in office.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Relevant experience supporting product and brand designers at Amazon (Fresh, AWS) and with large groups of stakeholders
- Clear passion for working with designers - went back to school to study UX design and decided in the end to go after Operations roles where they can enable designers to focus on their design work. Cares about quality of onboarding, engagement, building direct relationships with the ICs, etc.
- Appeared empathetic, transparent, proactive, rigorous and positive
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Unsure if they have experience supporting researchers
- Unsure how scrappy they might be able to be when not working in a huge org
interview_rounds.thoughts: Overall seems like a solid candidate for this role, with relevant experience on Amazon Fresh and AWS. Work and values seem to align well with company principles. In terms of leveling, thinking high DPM5 rather than DPM6. Background: DPM at Amazon Fresh (after AWS) - 2 years, PM on AWS (2 years), Program manager (3y).

Background: Head of Creative at Asana (1.5y), ECD at Zendesk (11y), 6-7y in agencies. Discussed multi-million dollar brand awareness campaign at Zendesk for AI positioning, worked with Ogilvy, fully integrated campaign (TV/Video, digital, OOH). Argued for brand awareness for 4-5 years at Zendesk.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Copywriting background
- Lots of B2B experience
- The origins for the "Zendesk Alternative" effort, grounded in SEO insights, was pretty awesome
- Answers to questions were great. Hearing about the strategy for the creative work on the Asana commercial was great. Philosophy on briefs was great too.
- Good sense of humor
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- A good chunk of the presentation delivery wasn't great. Ethan sounded nervous and compensating with humor that wasn't always landing, and reading a script. Things got better towards the end, and the Q&A was for sure, much better
- Aesthetic of Asana work feels very similar to Zendesk.
- Some comments like "I always deliver on the brief", etc made me wonder if Ethan had too strong of an ego. Q&A did make it sound much better.
- A lot of the collaboration with marketing was framed as a "us versus them"

name: Geoff Kim
debrief_notes: Principles: Geoff is a lovely human being – but I'm really struggling to see them successful in this role. The gap between their existing skillset, interest, and approach to the work, and our expectations for the role and on our Design team is just too wide. Portfolio: I'm struggling to put a 3 here, because there just isn't enough that felt convincing. Sometimes the work didn't feel super fleshed out, sometimes the process or role wasn't super well articulated, and it's unclear to me whether C could easily build campaigns for us outside of video ones.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Wide set of experiences with different brands (Roblox, beyonce, nike, etc) and medias
- lots of video/motion and photography experience
- good hand-drawn illustrations/type work and motion
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Case studies felt too concise. We didn't hear a lot about process, role, explorations, etc. and had to ask lots of questions to understand better.
- Some of the work lacked polish. For example, the Roblox video creator story had bad composition (cropped legs), some parts of the first Roblox video looked unfinished/unpolished
- Not a lot of experience working on more traditional B2B campaigns, or brands similar to Plaid with a narrower approach, with far less video/motion, etc. Unclear how easily they'd adapt to this type of work.
interview_rounds.thoughts: I'm struggling to put a 3 here, because there just isn't enough that felt convincing. Sometimes the work didn't feel super fleshed out, sometimes the process or role wasn't super well articulated, and it's unclear to me whether C could easily build campaigns for us outside of video ones. The fact that I'm so hesitant on even getting to a 3 makes me land on 2.

interview_rounds.did_well: - Seem able to move fast when needed to deliver on a tight timeline
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Didn't show a lot of interest for what we at Plaid consider "impact" > no mention of the effectiveness or their work, the metrics or goals, etc
- Didn't show a lot of ownership mentality - couldn't highlight a single project where they could have done better, and instead blame the founder's involvement
- C didn't talk much about quality, craft, producing great work.
- C didn't seem to understand how different it is to design for a B2B audience. When asked how they would approach the work differently, all they said was that they wanted to bring some fun to the "boring fintech sector" and that our brand was exciting
- C doesn't seem to what we'd consider a multidisciplinary brand designer - in fact, they explicitly kept referring to brand designers as a separate team and considered themselves a motion designer.
- C mostly uses Figma to gather moodboards and align with brand designers on concepts in Figjam
- Main motivation to join Plaid are to be on set, working with photography, etc (which they'll do some of in H2, but maybe not as much in 2026) and not having to commute to San Mateo (for Roblox). No mention of our mission or anything related to Plaid.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Geoff is a lovely human being - but I'm really struggling to see them successful in this role. The gap between their existing skillset, interest, and approach to the work, and our expectations for the role and on our Design team is just too wide.

Discussed brand narrative framework (positioning, context, differentiators, distinct values), Shopify Plus brand positioning challenges, getting executive buy-in, balancing brand building with performance marketing, cross-functional partnerships with Product and Design, handling misalignment across functions. Noted Plaid needs bigger brand investment than perf marketing. Fan of formalizing collaboration and responsibilities/ownership.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Director/editor/animator
- Seems to be curious and interested in experimenting, and creative
- Seems to think a lot about her narrative before production
- Willing to kill her ideas if they don't work (e.g. the pieces of paper turning into a doll)
- Seems able to help across a wide range of things
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Storytelling was too rich. Too detailed and hard to follow at times
- Cinematography/camera movement on those videos didn't feel super strong.
- Overall craft didn't feel super strong / didn't seem to think about that too much
- Had quite a big crew at Asana when shooting. Really only focused on directing talent and editing

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Strong partnership/collaboration with Design / At Cashapp, that's the name of the game, with designers now writing PRDs.
- Hannah has a background supporting designers directly and understands how to leverage the partnership. She shared multiple examples of how she's leveraged a deep partnership with Design to augment her team's impact.
- Thinks first about brand narrative from a product perspective (unsurprisingly given her experience in PMM) - shared the example of a recent product research insight at Cashapp highlighting that the best product to cement the brand perception of Cashapp as a banking solution was Borrow, their micro-loans feature. That insight drove an effort to reposition Cashapp + target a more specific audience - modern/independent earners, without traditional W2 income, etc.
- As a PMM, believes that half of the job is about output (how the product is positioned, G2M, etc), the other half is input (competitive analysis, customer insights/feedback, advocating for what will win in the market, etc).
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Just inherited brand strategy at Cashapp after brand strategist left - seems to have limited direct experience there overall but good experience collaborating with brand strategists. Unsure she could drive towards a clear vision without hiring one.
- Didn't mention performance marketing, metrics, business goals, etc during our chat.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Cashapp - product marketing, brand strategy. Affirm - led most of marketing. FB/Apple - also has done design. Working with head of design at Cashapp.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Builder, creative, wants to reach people and bring emotion and intentionality to their work.
- High energy and positivity, good sense of humor, seem very easy to work with
- Team building and mentorship are clearly important to them
- Solid bar raiser and coach, able to play bad cop if needed, and can think through all aspects of the work (brief, feedback, talent, relationships, comms, etc) to improve outcomes
- Experience running a full rebrand and working tightly with Product on it
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: VP Brand - Velocity Global (1y) - 30 person team, 4.5y at Amazon (CD on Amazon and Prime brand, content manager on Alexa). Discussed experience with product teams, rebrand at Velocity Global incorporating motion and 'Expand your orbit' into product. Gave honest assessment of Plaid brand: strong logo, illustration style needs to go, brands with tension are strong, solid foundation but system isn't translating personality and strategy.

Transition check-in notes: How are you feeling? And the team? Opportunity for better partnership across design. The rebrand is a unique opportunity. What can I do to make this transition as smooth as possible and the team successful?

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Very relevant experience in Banking/Payments products. Is actively integrating Plaid into Square's offerings
- Experience working on consumer-facing and customer-facing products
- Good system thinker
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Still very fresh in leadership and mentorship experience - not enough for this role
- Haven't really had a chance to lead other designers on projects, and be in a real leadership role
- Haven't worked outside of an established design system for a long time, and as they self-identified, expect to be rusty when it comes to coming up with new interaction design patterns, etc
- Doesn't have a very strong business acumen and one of the reasons they're looking at new opportunities is that Square is increasingly focusing on their business needs (the fact that they see that as negative is a bit of a red flag at this level)
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: 6y at Square (POS team, Square Banking - led Square Savings, Umbrella, Square Loans), 1.5y at Xero, 9y in SF now moved to NY. Majored in Industrial Design. Discussed Square Savings product, Square Banking unified experience, Loans Offer page (75% of Square Loans revenue), Cash Flow/P&L vision work, convincing leadership on design principles vs Legal requirements.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Really solid craft. Final output looked stunning
- Great creative direction / output from vendor was really well executed and connected well with strategy
- Project goals and success criteria were pretty clear
- Solid presenter. Clear, concise, well put together deck
- Thoughtful work overall. Solid intentionality behind the design decisions
- Can prototype, clearly cares about micro-interactions (though we didn't see many of them)
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- I wish we had seen more design explorations to see how Ian gets to the final output
- We didn't hear too much about design system/components and making work that can be reused elsewhere.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Solid web design experience, including working on challenging and ambiguous project with high level stakeholders, like the redesign of the Rippling homepage
- Cares about craft, about pushing himself, and working with people who are better than him so he can challenge himself and grow
- Curious, open-minded and adaptable
- Owns his mistakes - shared how he completely forgot to send a storyboard to their animator for a component of the new Careers page at Rippling, and it resulted in having to ship a static asset instead, which wasn't really telling the story.
- Has contributed to building the web design component system at Rippling (team of 3 web designers)
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- During our chat, the candidate never talked about business goals or audience needs
- A lot of the answers felt very shallow and it was hard to get good signal - as an example, to the question "what is a goal you set for yourself (not assigned by someone else) and how did you go about achieving it?", their answer was "learning new skills. I love to learn new skills".
- Didn't display a great ownership mentality: often blamed engineering capabilities as the reason for tradeoffs, or the quality of the final output
- Didn't exemplify a strong Championship team mentality - when asked what's an example of critical feedback they've given to someone, they shared: "When I was a manager, I provided performance reviews and advised a visual-based designer to consider more UI/UX aspects and look at resources like Pinterest for inspiration".

name: Icy Leng
debrief_notes: Portfolio: Good concepting and execution. Seem pretty scrappy. Good interaction/motion design. But not great at framing intentionality/decision-making – arguments seemed shallow. No mentions of design system. Goals seemed hand-wavy. Didn't see a lot of wide explorations. There are some spikes (pretty technical), but overall work lacks intentionality, divergence, wasn't grounded in business goals or metrics. Don't feel confident this C would hit the ground running. Principles: Pretty broad skillset, able to work fast, seem to care about craft. But background is only in small startups, haven't developed the 'championship team' muscle, still growing strategic skills, don't seem very impact oriented. A lot of the answers felt very shallow. Based solely on this interview, I don't feel confident that this candidate can raise the bar on the team.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- good concepting and execution. work was pretty engaging.
- pretty scrappy (e.g. learned spline to render a 3D version of the cube)
- Good interaction/motion design.
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- not great at framing their intentionality / decision-making. Arguments seemed pretty shallow in thinking ("to make it more cool...").
- There was no mentions of how other parts of the website/ecosystem worked, or a design system.
- goals seemed a little hand-wavy rather than metric-driven, and seemed to have been decided afterwards
- We didn't see a lot of wide explorations
- Answer to process & collaboration was ambiguous
- Projects were built with Webflow, and with motion designer + visual designer involved and a brand director doing the creative direction, so hard to know where their impact really was.
- In scrolling through the Tapcart homepage, it's clear where the visual designer was involved and where they weren't / and the quality of the craft isn't the same where they weren't.
interview_rounds.thoughts: There are some spikes, in particular the fact that the C is pretty technical. However, overall, their work lacks intentionality, divergence, wasn't super grounded in business goals or metrics. Combined with their lack of experience working on a web design team, with other designers, a design system, etc I don't feel confident this C would hit the ground running and be impactful right away at Plaid.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Codes
- Also helps the motion designer with storyboarding
interview_rounds.thoughts: Discussed launching new homepage in 6 days, using AI tools, making components reusable. Wants to join bigger team after only doing startups. Attracted to Plaid's design team's work.

name: Ivan Marinkovic
debrief_notes: Very easy to talk to, articulate, friendly, thoughtful. Very relevant experience at Wise. Has a bit of management experience. Has worked as a content designer. Seems self-aware and thoughtful. Actual product design experience is a bit on the shorter hand (just above two years), but their previous roles in Content Design have given them the opportunity to build a robust skillset. Let's bring Ivan for the final round!

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Very easy to talk to, articulate, friendly, thoughtful
- Very relevant experience at Wise.
- Has a bit of management experience
- Has worked as a content designer
- Seems self-aware and thoughtful
interview_rounds.did_not_well: - Actual product design experience is a bit on the shorter hand (just above two years, really), but their previous roles in Content Design have given them the opportunity to build a robust skillset.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Mo.work, Wise (7y). At Wise: started in CX, became UX writer (2y), managed team of 4 UX writers, helped launch in Japan, led design for Bank account details feature. At Mo: large traditional companies, had to leave for personal health issues. Let's bring Ivan for the final round!

name: Jacquelynn Gothard
debrief_notes: I'm a soft no hire – nothing I heard was a huge red flag, but I did come out of this conversation not feeling very confident that Jacque would be successful at Plaid as the sole content designer (for a little while) and setting the right bar for future hires.

interview_rounds.thoughts: Follow-up interview covering autonomy as sole content designer, working with many products and teams, prioritization, Meta voice and tone project. At Celigo: only CD for a year, increased 30-day trial retention by 16% through content improvements. Built style guide/voice guidelines. At Meta: led redefining voice and tone across family of Apps, from discovery to implementation.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Solid fintech experience
- Easy to talk to, pretty articulate
- Clearly excited about Plaid and the role (Moneybox uses Plaid)
- Clear appetite for product thinking, problem-solving, system-thinking
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Skeptical of their leadership skills and ability to work autonomously, prioritize their work to maximize their impact, and to manage stakeholders efficiently
- Skeptical of their business acumen - part of their reasons for leaving Moneybox today is a push for profitability, which shows a lack of clear understanding of how a business operates.
- By the way they talked about actual design execution, worry this C wouldn't enjoy a lot of the work this role also entails.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Moneybox (1y), Tide (2y), Freelance. While I really enjoyed my chat with James, I don't feel strongly enough that he'd be successful in this role.

name: Jason Martell
debrief_notes: Pre-screen: Lot of Fintech experience, super solid prototyper, 'Show, don't tell' approach, mentorship/teaching experience, good energy. Room for growth in XFN-collaboration. Portfolio: I'm a soft hire on Jason. Their skillset matches well our 'The Hybrid' D7 archetype – someone with strong technical craft and execution skills. However, very little signal on business acumen, and their presentation lacked structure. Final Round: Jason is a passionate designer, with many years of experience, including in Fintech, and a great appetite for Plaid. Their hard skills are great. While we won't find the perfect unicorn, this candidate's T-shaped skillset leans too heavily on their hard skills. Not very humble, struggle to collaborate, still growing their leadership skills. I'm not confident they'd be successful in this role.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Lot of Fintech experience
- Good balance of startup and large company exp
- Has been in a horizontal role at Meta, working on a "product architecture" team that floats around the company and partners with other teams
- Super solid prototyper - mostly designs in Origami
- "Show, don't tell" approach to the work > will make a demo before making a doc or deck to explain something
- Mentorship/teaching experience (mostly in tools and systems)
- Good energy & excitement for the role
- Stays current, in touch with new tech (AI, etc)
- Transparent and self-aware: were open to sharing real failures and what they learned, and what they aren't great at today
interview_rounds.did_not_well: - Room for growth in XFN-collaboration (esp. at Meta): keeping stakeholders in the loop, having right process around touchpoint, etc.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Meta (3y) - Design systems, Evils (1y) - Design systems, Acorns (3y) early stage designer. 15 years in Fintech. Co-founder Acorns. Biggest failure: Blast - no product market fit. Greatest weakness: cross-functional unified collaboration.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Very strong spike in craft and execution. They fit well our "The Hybrid" archetype of someone with strong technical craft and execution. Very strong prototyper and visual designer that can think systematically and across many different products and surfaces.
- Real hacking mentality - loves to nimble with side projects, explore ideas, prototype them and create components out of those they can reuse later in new projects
- Great experience working on cross-team, cross-functional initiatives at Meta.
- "Show, don't tell" approach to their work - have produced tons of demos for internal stakeholders to get them rallied behind their ideas and push their work forward
- Deep consumer-facing interaction design XP at Meta, with co-presence or profile switching, at a system-level
- The work they presented was very visual and engaging. Jason leverages those skills to communicate their vision internally really well.
- Thoughtful work - e.g. when answering Sneha's questions around intensity of some of their explorations, candidate had a whole system and principles in place to review intensity levels of components and how they impact attention
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- The presentation jumped a little bit all over the place. Lacked some structure and better context setting. They focused on showing us a lot of work (which clearly, they've produced a ton of) but more structure and context-setting + better articulation of their role would have been.
- Not a lot of business goals in their work at Meta (typical for Meta).
- I would have wanted them to tell us more about their past experience at Acorns and other Fintech startups.
- Could have given us clearer answers to some of our questions about engagement models with other teams, prioritization, influencing decision-making, etc. Will need to dive into those 1:1.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Very easy to talk to 1:1
- "Show, don't tell" approach carried through to this 1:1 meeting where they almost always had something to show me to illustrate their point.
- Real appetite for the role and the space. They know Plaid well, and understand the industry and the opportunity
- Self-aware on how they did in the presentation: brought up at the beginning that they regretted not having shared more of their process and got too excited about showing the work.
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Not very humble. A lot of "I", very little "we". Almost no mention of their partners, even when prompted to talk about them. When asked about their biggest failure, mentioned having been CPO of an early-stage startup that failed and didn't get any traction, yet said "we did nothing wrong". Their only learning was that failure is part of the process, but they didn't have any learning on what they could have done differently
- Work great in a silo, but struggle to collaborate. Have had issues dealing with feedback from their peers or XFN partners. Seem to value their intuition and principles above any feedback they receive.
- Still growing their leadership skills - I couldn't get them to give me concrete examples on how they build influence, what decisions they helped make, what issues they identified and helped fixed on a team, etc.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Debrief summary: Decided not to extend an offer. Jason is a candidate who's clearly spiking in execution, and in solving system-level problems across different products and surfaces. They also have a good hacking/scrappy mentality which would work well at Plaid. However, we have concerns across the board about their communication, collaboration, and leadership skills, they didn't give us a clear sense of their process/approach to solving problems, and their business acumen. Those are all important aspects of the skillset needed to succeed in this role, and Jason's gaps in those seem too deep.

name: Jason Rosenberg
debrief_notes: I'm a very, very soft hire – if Chris is impressed with Jason as a leader and feels confident that they'll lead the team successfully in this new phase, I won't block us from moving to the next stage. I feel sufficiently confident that I can work with Jason well in nurturing the Product <> Brand culture (though I'll likely have to lean into it a lot more) but have a few concerns I'd want us to check into in future rounds if we move forward with Jason.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- The work they've led at Bolt is really strong. And super relevant to what we're doing at Plaid.
- They've worked closely with Product Design during that rebrand, sweating the details so it feels cohesive (they mentioned incorporating certain special characters from the new brand into the product experience, for example), and have since been working closely with Head of PD to bring the teams closer together.
- Easy to talk to, curious, honest and self-aware (they mentioned that this role at Plaid would stretch them as they've never led a team this big, but they're excited about the opportunity)
- Genuinely excited about the role and opportunity
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Their experience has primarily been at agencies. Bolt, for the last 1.5 year, is their only experience working "in-house" and with a product team.
- Clearly most motivated by the fact that we're doing a rebrand (which was also their motivation for joining Bolt). I'm concerned that they'd jump ship to the next opportunity the minute the rebrand is launched.
- No experience leading a team of this size.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Bolt (1.5y) - Head of Creative, Pareira O'Dell (agency - 3.5y) - Creative Director, Eleven inc (Agency, 3y) - Associate Creative Director. Discussed tight partnership with Product Design at Bolt during rebrand, managing cultural differences between marketing and product design teams, up-leveling team work through audits/education/feedback/mentorship.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Incredible pedigree as a product leader, having led product at various types of businesses and growing them to $100M+ revenue.
- Clear founder mentality (having founded multiple companies before)
- Fantastic partner to design. Their experience at Yammer, at Airbnb, or Do.com (their own company) has taught them a lot about how to build a strong PM/Design partnership and a culture around shipping high quality products people love using.
- They also have a clear love and curiosity for design and craft (they shared the example of an eraser they recently encountered while on a trip in Japan that was in a very odd shape, and as they looked into why, realized it was actually much more practical than a regular eraser).
- Pragmatic, thoughtful and calm, principled and passionate
- Very clear and easy to talk to
- Did really well in my mini-case study on RM enrollment.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: - Can't think of anything during this chat.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Founded company in education (2005), Yammer, Do.com, Amazon, Airbnb (pre-IPO, grew new businesses from 0 to $100M revenue), Alchemy (scaled 25 to 200, $20M to $100M revenue). Discussed quality culture at Airbnb, PM/Design partnership, Yammer chat challenge. Interested in how Plaid goes from dev platform to consumer network.

name: Jennifer Hung
