debrief_notes: Overall I'm a soft hire. No red flags, but also nothing making me super excited from this quick conversation. Their motion design skills/experience, combined with business mind and process obsession would all be helpful adds, but I don't yet have strong confidence they'd uplevel the work of our team overall.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Very process-oriented and organized (30/60/90 day plan shared with everyone)
- Solid experience overall
- Result oriented, strive to go beyond her goals
- Solid motion-design background and expertise
- Overall seems like a solid partner to Product, though she looks at the relationship as Product being a client of Brand
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Their answers on how they level up the quality of their team's work were very process-oriented (which is part of the solution, but not the only part)
- Jennifer's assessment of the current Plaid brand didn't resonate a lot with me. They said they thought the Plaid brand was stronger than Stripe's, and that it just needed to be more conservative/serious (without going all the way to what a bank looks like) which isn't necessarily wrong, but didn't sound very inspired.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Based in LA, born in Hawaii. Was at Uber 3y, Amazon, HBO Max, Shopify, Square. Lots of campaign work. Was animator/visual effects, led video at NY Times. Discussed 180 days of change at Uber, HBO Max App Store video (30%+ increase in downloads), process improvements at Square. Assessment of Plaid brand: more personality than Stripe, illustration style feels too juvenile, visual identity could be refined.

interview_rounds.did_well: - Experience as a creative (started as a designer themselves) and in working with creatives - good instincts for how to drive their performance, give them the right level of clarity and space, etc.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Slack (1y), Khoros (3.5y), Lagos (4y), NBA (4y) as art director+graphic designer. A lot of DTC brands, last 5 years focused on Tech. At Khoros: rebuilt team post-layoffs, grew to 12 in 3 months, kept team for 2+ years. At Slack: brand identity was all over the place, worked on centering brand and training company post-Salesforce acquisition. Assessment of Plaid brand: strong elements, opportunities for rigor and structure, needs a haircut but not a complete overhaul.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Experience of being pretty scrappy, building side projects (including an NFT marketplace/app)
- Good technical background. Has used our API to build stuff
- Solid blue-sky thinking. Creative, passionate, and in touch with emerging industry trends
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Borderline pretentious. A lot of "I made that, I did that, everyone loves what I did here". Very few mentions of teams. I had a few questions planned for them before talking about any work, but they took over and started walking me through some of their work unprompted. Mostly fine on its own, but combined with some of the language they used, I worry about the ego this C would bring to the day-to-day work.
- Visual craft - the products this C showed me didn't highlight a high level of attention to detail and overall quality
- Leadership experience - though the candidate mentioned reporting into the Head of Product of Wing.com, they really struggled to articulate how they manage the relationship with leaders/stakeholders, how they prioritize their work, how they mentor others, etc
- Business acumen - at no point did the candidate talk about the business impact or considerations of their work, and only focused on the user experience.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Unfortunately, this candidate seemed promising on paper but is falling short in a few important places (craft, leadership and business acumen) and I worry about their interpersonal skills (and ego) for this role (that will require building trust with other designers in a timely manner when collaborating with them, etc). Background: Stanford d-school, CS background, Google, Intuit, startups. Created folio (NFT app). GoogleX - designed OpenSky app, built design system as extension of Material Design.

name: John Bai
recommendation: 4 - Strong Hire (pre-screen), 4 - Strong Hire (portfolio), 4 - Strong Hire (final round)
debrief_notes: Pre-screen: John is great. Let's bring them to the next step! Fintech experience at Brex, great consumer-facing experience at Lyft and Dropbox, very solid craft, great prototyper using Framer, solid business acumen, great communicator, curious and thoughtful. Portfolio: Fintech experience, very solid craft, great prototyper, solid business acumen and user centricity. Final Round: John is overall great. They're earlier in the level than some other candidates we've seen, but their growth areas are clear and easily coachable. I already can picture where they'd plug in on day 1 to be successful. Great startup mentality, great communicator, great self-awareness.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Fintech experience (Brex) - built a full spend management feature set that's super well executed from 0-1, turning it into a big part of the Brex ecosystem
- Great consumer-facing experience at Lyft and Dropbox + experience building B2B tools at Brex and Carta
- Very solid craft
- Great prototyper, using Framer
- Solid business acumen
- Great communicator. Articulate, engaging, visual
- Leads by example and raises the bar through their work and feedback
- Producing engaging loom videos to share what works for him or how he approaches problems to impact his team
- Curious, thoughtful and asking great questions
interview_rounds.did_not_well: - Not a lot of signal on leadership skills, but the little I got was positive
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Brex 2.5y, Lyft (Rider team), Dropbox Paper, Carta. Discussed Brex mobile app redesign initiative, mentoring junior designers. Looking for growth and development, 0-1 work, complex problems.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Experience working on large teams (was leading a team of 30 at Ancestry, while directly managing 7), on large products/companies (recently at Twitter), but also on 0-1 products (True, agency work)
- Seem comfortable adapting to fast-paced environments or working on longer timeframes
- Solid user-centricity
- Solid mentorship and management experience
- Self-identified superpower of taking in lots of different inputs/data/knowledge, making sense of all of it, and repackaging it in a simple and engaging way
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- They have been let go twice in their last 3 jobs (Ancestry, Twitter) and rage-quitted at True when the company decided to return to the office. Not the greatest signal
- Not a lot of signal on how good they are at execution, prototyping, etc. Some of the work on their website looks solid, but was made in large parts through others at Ancestry (when they were managing) and they themselves mentioned having really only gotten back into producing designs themselves at Twitter.
- Fairly weak answers on how they manage stakeholders and their experience working with leaders
- Some lack of self-awareness (or transparency): couldn't really answer what was the best piece of feedback they received in the last year
- Very low energy. Didn't do a great job of showing their excitement for the role or for being considered.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Sr Staff PD at Twitter 9m, VP Design for True (social media startup), Director of UX at Ancestry for 3.5 years (30+ team). Studied CS and HCI. Discussed Ancestry Mosaic product, frustrations at True with CEO involvement, Twitter pace. Personal connection to Plaid mission (grew up in large, poor family).

name: Josh Stein
debrief_notes: Portfolio: Super strong presentation. Great work, great delivery, and relevant experience. Probably more of a mid-high D5. Principles: Great candidate. Maybe a bit over-experienced for the role as we've set it up – but would be immediately able to contribute in a meaningful way.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Good communicator. Funny, clear, engaging.
- Work in advertising agency, doing tone of voice for brands (Android, Disney+, etc)
- Experience being the first content designer at Better Mortgage and Guideline - wrote guidelines, brand book, etc, hired and managed people
- Really relevant Fintech experience at Better Mortgage and Guideline
- Ran their own research to guide their work
- Principled - anchoring business needs into people problems to guide the work
- Clear in their process, well organized, including in how they support different teams
- Concept of the content funnel was very interesting and really helped structure their work on Better Mortgage
- Led to really elegant designs
- And that work led to real business impact
- Seem easy to work with, collaborative
interview_rounds.did_not_well: - Didn't really mention principles/guidelines and how they impacted their work by themselves
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: NYC. Guideline (retirement platform): 1.5y - Staff. Better (mortgage): 2.5 years (incl. 9 months of player coach). First content designer.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Collaborative, pragmatic, with solid business understanding.
- Mentorship and management experience.
- Self-starter: as they want to grow their mentorship skills, with the goal to be a head of design one day, C has started a shadowing program where members of the Sales and Customer Success teams can shadow them for a quarter + get direct feedback on their email templates, etc.
- Displayed a solid maturity in detaching their personal feelings from the outcome of decision-making or experiments, and seemed ok to be proven wrong or for decisions to not go their way
- Shared how they're always exploring at least 3 versions of everything, even if they don't share them, as a practice to always be diligent
- Self-aware: mentioned they have a tendency to let the "we'll do a fast-follow" excuse get in the way of them pushing back on a sub-par experience, and have received the feedback that they should share their opinion more often.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Discussed Better Mortgage closing experience vision (starting from brand promise), created shadowing program for Sales/CS, ran experiment splitting email vs product notifications for tax forms comms (product notifications won). Sees self as high-level IC in 2 years, less interested in management politics, misses being close to the work.

name: Judy Zhong
debrief_notes: Principles: Judy seems to have a really solid all-around skillset combined with very strong growth potential. She seems to have learned a ton in the few years of experience she's had so far, and demonstrated clear appetite to continue growing and tackling bigger problems while sharpening her skills. Portfolio: Might be on the earlier side of IC5 given the years of experience, but really solid candidate with a great all-around skillset, and growth potential.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Eng background
- Experience in the insurance space, working with underwriters
- Experience working for specific audiences: Small business ads at Meta, people team at Square, merchant at Doordash, etc
- Great presenter / high energy, engaging, great storytelling
- Solid prototyping + curious about Cursor, etc
- data and user centric. Work is grounded in actionable metrics and clear user insights
- Good articulation of their design thinking / intentionality
- Shared explorations that didn't make the cut
interview_rounds.thoughts: Might be on the earlier side of IC5 given the years of experience, but really solid candidate with a great all-around skillset.

Discussed Microsoft Labs Surface Duo project (designing in vacuum), manager at Doordash as inspiration, getting better at asking questions in 0-1 projects, feedback about knowing the metrics. Goal: be known as the craft person (visual craft), practices 1 prototype a day with Lovable. At Robinhood: struggled to distinguish discoverability vs accessibility, felt like a design monkey.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Solid experience supporting creative teams at Fitbit (5y), Headspace (1y) and Zipline (4y)
- working directly with CMO/Marketing leads and creative lead
- Built into Asana a way to visualize workload on the team and tracking who the projects came from, the size of the projects, etc, leading to strategic conversations about resourcing and priorities across business lines
- Clearly likes data visualization (e.g. gantt charts, dashboards, etc) as a way to get a sense for what's cooking and what are the consequences of shifting priorities
- Experience finding/onboarding/leveraging external agencies/vendors to support creative team, including for video
- Experience building a video studio at Zipline
- Built an Airtable to visualize all the assets being built/tested
- Problem solver / chief of staff to the head of Marketing
interview_rounds.did_not_well: - At times, the presentation felt like a textbook presentation and felt theoretical (vs grounded in examples)

interview_rounds.thoughts: Final round questions prepared covering operational problem-solving, quick decisions with consequences, unrealistic deadlines, program acceleration, aligning Design/Product/Marketing, communication tailoring, healthy design org signals, designer morale, and process philosophy. No evaluations recorded.

name: Justin Kim
debrief_notes: Portfolio: Justin is way too junior for what we're looking for in this role. Very minimal experience, storytelling showed lack of mature product thinking, demos/prototypes were very basic, answers were very rambly and hand-wavy. Principles: Very hand-wavy answers, did not really own up their mistakes, their way of balancing business impact and user needs is very much still developing, have mostly operated as sole designer.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Pretty good storytelling/context setting (though long and rambly)
- Visual design is reasonably good, including a bit of system design (though for only themselves)
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Very minimal experience. Really started as a Product designer in 2020, which includes 9 months supporting Plaid as a production designer. No experience as a Product Designer at a company of the size and scale of Plaid.
- Storytelling on Enzo was more about company strategy pivot than really a big pivot in product. Project was very small in scope. Framing and answers to questions showed lack of mature product thinking.
- Shift to a 4% interest rate was launched with a small in-app modal. Didn't feel like the "big pivot" it was framed as. Treatment was quite subtle and unlikely to affect customer perception.
- Demos/prototypes were very basic click-through and lacked the level of polish/visual refinement we look for (for an IC5)
- C talked about a lot of small iterative changes that led to a massive growth, though we didn't see them.
- Answers to questions were very rambly and hand-wavy.
- Made some pretty surface-level comments like "black was feeling too cold, so we made the button blue"

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Have a reasonably solid design process, with double-diamond divergence/convergence, etc.
- Appear to care about the customers, their needs, and their jobs to be done, though they don't always incorporate those into their work.
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Very hand-wavy answers to questions.
- Did not really own up their mistakes and passed the responsibilities to "the founder" or other stakeholders for their shortcoming.
- Their way of balancing business impact and user needs is very much still developing. In our chat, they only talked about business impact in a negative light, and as a reason to cut down on the user experience they wanted to deliver.
- Have mostly operated as sole designer, and lack experience in working with other designers on complex projects, raising the bar, etc
- Though their visual design chops appear reasonably solid, I don't think they're at a level yet where they can really push beyond our existing system and sweat the details to deliver highly polished experiences.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Very hand-wavy overall.

name: Kiran Puri
debrief_notes: Principles: I came out of this interview a very soft 3. I had a very pleasant chat with Kiran, heard promising answers, that were at times contrasted with small red flags. Overall, no big red flags but I also don't feel strongly that this candidate would be a huge addition to our team. Portfolio: I'm a soft 3. The relevant experience is a plus, and the execution skills look solid – I was just craving a bit more to feel more strongly.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Experience building campaigns for developers
- Clear process, with lots of concepts explored (and shared)
- The work shared was overall solid and well presented
- Solid success metrics
- Some motion design skills
- Conducted audience research with two product managers
- Led a team-wide design sprint for idea generation, involving 14 team members to contribute to the rebrand
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Nothing had the wow factor. The work was overall solidly executed, but felt overall pretty safe
- On the bigger project (developer rebrand), it was a bit unclear (to me, at least) what came from the candidate versus the ACD on the project or the other designers. For example, it sounds like the push for the 3D icons came from the ACD
- Overall, there was less need for stakeholder management and convincing others to try new ideas
- Less clear how deep their expertise is with video creative direction. They mentioned having been on set and helping out, but didn't share much detail
interview_rounds.thoughts: I'm a soft 3. The relevant experience is a plus, and the execution skills look solid / I was just craving a bit more to feel more strongly. Side notes: Mentioned being interested in the role at Plaid in parts because it's in SF (not the greatest signal). Left Square a year ago - what have they done since?

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Love thinking about brand systems, how to scale them, etc. Capable of synthesizing information in a helpful way due to systemic thinking.
- Pragmatic: will sacrifice going all-in on a concept if it ensures the final delivery is at a better quality level
- Seem pretty self-aware - lack of confidence in their own design skills can get in the way of them pushing their POV
- Recognizes a tendency to be shy but aims for designs to be bold, especially in initial phases to drive ideas forward.
- Tinkering with AI (ChatGPT/Midjourney) for concepting
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Mentioned having trouble knowing when to move from the conceptual phase to execution. Love tinkering with concepts, but can get stuck there. Though I appreciate the honesty and self-awareness, this feels like a small red flag for an IC5 as they might require oversight. They mentioned having made progress on this topic recently.
- Mentioned a few times that they feel quite strong during the conceptual phase, but struggle a bit more with visual design execution. Though it didn't show in the work we saw, I worry this might be an actual challenge we didn't pick up during portfolio because they had other designers contributing. It could also be that they have a high bar and don't feel like they're meeting their own expectations yet (hard to tell)
interview_rounds.thoughts: Discussed developer brand project, Google IO social systems, Android/Play developers landing page, Women in Techmakers brand update. Inspired by colleague Jeremy C's attention to craft details (shadows). Getting better at moving from concept to execution. Using AI for creative explorations.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Solid experience building for consumers, at Catch, Cash App, Candidly
- Solid experience working with Design. Their partner, and co-founder of Catch, is a designer and they've always elevated the role of design in the work they've done
- Good approach on how to balance quality and building new features.
- Thoughtful, pragmatic. Loves bringing the right tension between PM and Design to make sure we're building the right thing, with the right goals and constraints
- Genuinely passionate about Fintech, our mission, and building for consumers
- At Catch, after a successful Series A in 2021, realized that they'd need to sell in 2022. Pitched a vision for the team on how to achieve an exit by refocusing on Health Insurance (highest Rev product, but with lowest usage). Convinced people to stay and achieve a great outcome.
- Very excited about Plaid. Interviewed with us in 2022 but decided to go to Cash app at the time because we weren't really focused on Consumers.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: - While the outcome of the vision they pitched at Catch had a great outcome and was rooted in transparency and feasibility, they didn't share a lot about the process to establish it. I don't have a lot of signal on how they're able to think long term (though I expect, as a company founder/CEO, they've had to do some of that when fundraising, at least).
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Product Lead at Cash App (8 mo), Catch (head of Product > COO > CEO, 5.5y), Candidly (director of product and ops). Discussed partnership with Design, quality philosophy (hierarchy of needs, MVPs with delight), tension between PM and Design. Case study on RM enrollment: questioned whether enrollment is the right metric, more interested in second-time usage, compared to Shop Pay experience.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Very easy to talk to, calm, articulated and clear
- Solid experience, both on B2C and at Saas companies, and both in-house and at agencies
- Good values and experience when it comes to establishing a brand strategy and bringing it to life, starting with the Why of the company's existence, turning it into a compelling story and persona, and then creating the visual expression.
- Resonated well with their answer to "what do you think of the Plaid brand today?"
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Some of their answer to my first few questions around building a team, or bringing accountability and driving performance, felt a little obvious and lacked some concrete examples. I had no red flags in those answers, however.
- Not sure they have the experience to manage a team of this size - but they're hoping to learn it quickly
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Gong (2.5y) Director of Brand and Creative, Optimizely (2.5y) Director of Brand and Creative, Art Director with B2C ad campaign + business degree. Strategic-minded, built a team at Optimizely + did a rebrand, versatile as a brand strategist.

name: Lilian Tellez
debrief_notes: Principles: Struggling to give a rating. Got very low signal on many dimensions. Their answers were a little too surface-level. Don't have strong conviction this C would help uplevel the team. Portfolio: Very low energy when presenting. Seems to lack strong POV/ambition with their design. Presentation skills are fairly important at Plaid – this is the second time I get to see this C present this work in the last 12 months, and had the same notes the first time, which is concerning.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Experience in fintech at PayPal and Moneylion
- Experience in designing at scale for both mobile/desktop and consumer/B2B (LinkedIn)
- Management and mentorship experience (though we didn't go super deep into it)
- Clear execution process
- Seems like an ecosystem thinker, with great user centricity.
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Not the most engaging speaker
- Projects they mentioned sounded all pretty focused in scope, and not a lot of impact beyond their direct area.
- Don't seem too big on motion/prototyping
interview_rounds.thoughts: I really didn't get a lot of signal from Lilian. Their presentation was a little slow and I wasn't super quick on my feet to calibrate my questions correctly, so it's partly my fault too. I'll give myself a C- in running this interview. Let's have them meet with a DM and get a second look.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Solid experience, on different types of problems with different audiences and within established design teams/cultures
- experience on high impact/visibility products
- good use of low-fi for early explorations and of prototyping
- Work overall reasonably polished (though clearly working out of an existing system)

interview_rounds.did_well:

interview_rounds.did_not_well:

interview_rounds.thoughts: Struggling to give a rating to this interview. Lilian and I had a good chat, yet overall I got very low signal on many of the dimensions. Their answers were a little too surface-level, and most examples felt a bit too shallow for me to really assess. Based on this interview alone, I don't have strong conviction this C would help uplevel the team / though if other interviews were all very solid, I wouldn't block a hiring decision based on this interview alone.

interview_rounds.did_well:

interview_rounds.did_not_well:

interview_rounds.thoughts: I'm a soft 3. I find Maggie's experience at MF&T super interesting, and she clearly has solid communication skills. However, I left her presentation feeling like I only got surface-level information, and don't know that I have all the signals to feel confident yet.

Maryam is very easy to talk to, articulate, curious and engaging. They bring a lot of experience in B2C checkout/booking products. They seem to enjoy prototyping and putting their ideas in front of users to validate them. They have a little bit of experience in managing people and in mentoring junior designers. Maryam's main motivation for a new role is focusing on their craft, which they said they only get to spend 70% of their time on today while managing.

Concerns: abilities to influence cross-functionally, to work with leadership, and to prioritize their own work. All answers to those questions were pretty vague and required a lot of thinking. Mentioned not really being part of the product strategy setting and the decision-making.

I'm a soft 3: if our candidates in the final round don't close and we feel really good about Maryam's execution skills, I'm down to bring them to the next stage.

interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Cash App - Product Lead - 4 years, Square - Data Science/ML Lead/GM

They'd be a good bet if we were hiring a mid-level IC4, but I don't see the signs of crazy fast growth that'd get them to the level we need in 6-12 months.

- Experience leading huge campaigns ($180M+) and helping company build and flex its brand muscle

- Thoughtful, passionate about enabling great creativity on a team and blossoming great brand ideas
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Doesn't have an online portfolio - mentioned they're more focused on leadership over individual contributions. Unsure what this means
- Self-reported growth area: using overly aspirational language that can be unclear; needs to balance this with practical communication

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Fintech experience at Coinbase & Square (including a Risk product at Square)
- DevX experience at Square
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Very low energy, not very engaging speaker
- Last 8-9y in leadership/management role (except a little bit of IC at Square)
- Signal on leadership experience was pretty weak
- Project shared was pretty small in scope, and not executed by them but by their direct reports.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: DM Coinbase (1y, Coinbase Cloud/Wallet), Square (4.5y, Financial Services/Square Developer), Le Tote (1.5y, HoD), Say (1.5y), Nurun/Odopod (5.5y, agency).
Well/poorly sections left empty.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- huge background at Twitter. Scaled up the creative studio to 100+, helped with IPO, then built up and led the Content team, focused on social media
- Leading creative at Chime. Came in and immediately challenged the new brand they were building, and moved it to a diff direction
- Has worked on big campaigns - superbowl ads, etc
- Aspiration to be a CMO - thinks about the work from both "art and science" perspective, and leans into the data, insights, etc
- Very connected to culture, and leverages that to make work that lands
- Strong storytelling/concept skills
- Big into AI. Has pushed it all the way on the Chime team
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Slight worry about ego. Hard to really get a full read in 1 chat, but some comments made me wonder (e.g. "I always wanna make the winning play", "I always want to be the one that is helping the team just win")
- Spent so much time talking about huge initiatives and campaigns, and has had huge budgets at Twitter and Chime, that I worry our scale may not be meeting him where he's at
interview_rounds.thoughts: Let's have Ryan meet with Chris and get a second read. Ryan obviously brings a very creative mindset, has big ideas and big ambitions, and a marketing muscle that seems well flexed. I have slight worries about a potential disconnect between the type of work he's led and the work we do here, and potentially too strong of a personality for our culture, but in both cases I have very teeny-tiny signal.
Well/poorly sections left empty.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Solid storytelling. Work was anchored around clear goals, metrics, user needs, etc
- Good approach to experimentation - Leads her team in exploring north stars, forming hypotheses
- Work presented was well crafted, with some solid prototyping and included some branding work
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Work presented leans quite consumer/prosumer, and very different from the day-to-day of what this role will be about
- Slight worry that the level of technical complexity and how different the audiences we design for on Dashboard are might be a steep learning curve for Sandhya.
- I wish we had seen more explorations that didn't make the cut
interview_rounds.thoughts: Sandhya seems like a fairly solid design manager/leader. No big red flags - but also nothing standing out super strongly.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- reasonable experience managing growth of senior designers, and dealing with a low performer. Definitely enough for an M5
- Empathetic, good listener, seem easy to work with and supportive leader
- Created craft guides at Instacart that capture what contributes to "craft" and how designers can progress on the different dimensions
- Part of the "fun committee" to help organize onsites, etc.
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- doesn't seem to have been put in a position where they have to really think about staffing/team allocation rigorously
- Answers on how they deal with XFN challenges/conflict felt pretty shallow.
- I couldn't get a good signal on how hands-on she is.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Sandhya seems to be a pretty solid people manager. I'm less confident she brings a lot of leadership/drive, and proactivity. Nothing in our discussion came out as a red flag - she'd be easy to manage by Joe and would follow directions. But I couldn't get a clear point of view, methodology, or experience that makes Sandhya appear as a clear value-add/level-up to the team. I don't see any clear spike.

name: Sarah Cantrell
debrief_notes: Portfolio: Some decent motion design experience/skills. Jack of all trades, able to handle the rebrand of a smaller company on their own and work directly with C-level executives. Clearly articulated goals. Explored diligently and shared some of their explorations. But craft isn't meeting our bar. Lack of attention to details throughout, including in presentation that had typos.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Dynamic, engaging, bubbly personality that makes it easy to build a relationship with
- Curious and passionate about innovation
- Very solid experience in building brand strategy, in particular at Google where they worked on a large variety of products and brands and helped establish the first brand guidelines, but also at a smaller scale at Nuro or BBH
- Good experience partnering with creatives - have learned their lessons on what works/what doesn't when partnering
- Solid experience partnering with Product teams. Best example is how they pushed the marketing efforts around Privacy and Security at Google into the product ecosystem
interview_rounds.did_not_well: - Though they're currently managing a small brand design team at Nuro, most of their experience has been in partnering with Brand Design. Managing a team like our Brand design team would be newer to them.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Berkeley, startups, agencies, BBH in London, Google 7y Global head of Brand Marketing, Nuro 1.5y VP Brand & Marketing.
Well/poorly sections left empty.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Pretty solid visual craft. Work showed was polished
- Jack-of-all-trades: print/OOH, digital, animation, basic product and 3D
- Showed lots of explorations on the second case study, which was great
- Seems to have appetite to learn and grow
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Quite early in their career - graduated in december and first gig post-graduation started 3 months ago. Even if candidates has done a bit of freelance work before art school, I'm skeptical of them being ready for L5.
- Almost no experience with video production.
- We didn't see a ton of digital/ad work
interview_rounds.thoughts: If we decide to go for an IC4 with potential, this candidate might be a good bet. But based on the portfolio presentation alone, I'm skeptical of them being successful in the role as we've been considering it - I don't see them running large campaigns fully autonomously without oversight. I'm a soft 2 though.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Clearly loves moving fast. When a project doesn't really have a timeline/deadline, Shakthi will create deadlines for himself. Used the metaphor of a Nascar driver: foot on the pedal until the checkered flag.
- Seems very disciplined with his work overall
- Clearly growth-oriented - spends 2-3h every morning learning new things/techniques
- Super easy to talk to, engaging, passionate
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- I can't tell how well Shakthi can operate in an environment where craft is so important
- if time isn't always of the essence, can he manage his time efficiently and not get lost (something he confessed is a weakness)
- Not 100% clear on what campaigns/projects he's directly led.
- Minor thing, but I'm a bit surprised that he'd want to leave his company after 2 months.

name: Shannon Riley
recommendation: 2 - No Hire (shadowing Rubi Santoro), 2 - No Hire (interview focuses)
debrief_notes: Interview Focuses: I didn't get a ton of signal in this interview (C tended to ramble a bit with their answers, which limited the number of questions I was able to ask) but what I got didn't make me feel confident they'd succeed in this role. Nothing was terrible, but very little stood out as a key strength they'd be adding to the team.

interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Was at Brex before (laid off in October), Meta DPM on Ads and Instagram. Joined Meta as first DPM in sub team on Ads. Helped build brand for Ads & Business product, managed rollout of rebrand, team built digital asset management tool. At Brex: framework for announcements process. Vision sprint at Meta didn't go well - brief wasn't clear, sprint went from 4 weeks to 2.5 months. Best feedback: ruthlessly prioritize. No formal well/poorly sections in this file.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Show don't tell mentality
- Leadership/management experience
- Collaborative. "Design is for everyone" inclusive approach to designing products. Use product teardowns as a way to include partners early on
- Pitched product ideas internally all the way to GA
- Ability to work in rapid iterations (4 day design, 1 week build, 1 day of test and shareout)
- Experience designing elegant dashboards for complex user flows
- User-centric design - deep understanding of their audience and their needs
- System-level thinking - their work is built around modules that can be surfaced in other parts of the product
- Fantastic execution. Deck itself was well executed, and the work was high quality throughout. Work is detail oriented
- Clear and articulate presenter and great storyteller
interview_rounds.did_not_well: - At times, and because of the complexity of the work they were presenting, the candidate gave too much detail, making their storytelling slightly less effective

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Growth and craft oriented - Really care about craft, pushing themselves and the product, and continue growing as a designer in all directions
- Ambitious. Excited about addressing big challenges and problems, even through the lens of smaller feature work.
- Own their mistakes and learn from them
- Appetite and high energy for pushing our dashboard into a new era.
interview_rounds.did_not_well: - I didn't get a lot of signal how customer-obsessed they are during this interview (however, I got plenty in their portfolio presentation)
interview_rounds.thoughts: Sell chat notes: considers herself mid career, wants right mentors, scope of work on dashboard, culture where you can be friends with coworkers, stability, opportunity for leadership role on IC route.

name: Sinjin Hilaski
debrief_notes: 1:1: C did well in this interview and seems aligned with our company values (though we only had 30 min so I couldn't go super deep). Portfolio: I'm overall a VERY soft Yes on the portfolio presentation, mostly because the candidate's work seems pretty solid, and their experience is quite relevant. However, I don't think they did amazingly well with their storytelling, and missed several opportunities to better context-set, show broad explorations, articulate their work and mention business needs. Caveat: I don't feel confident about their craft and execution. I'm a soft Yes if others had a solid signal on it and feel positive, if not I'm a No.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Work seems solid overall
- In the Atlassian case study, they showed good intentionality behind their work.
- Leveraged UXR feedback on their work at Atlassian to inform Tone of voice
- Experience in dealing with complex features that lean heavily on content to educate and create clarity
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Storytelling: At times, went a little quick and missed an opportunity to strengthen their context setting
- Often focused on explaining the technical complexity behind the features rather than their work on language itself
- At times, C got lost in explaining their reasoning, and not always with visuals/content to support the narrative
- Didn't show many explorations, and rarely explained the why between the choice of certain words/language
- Not a lot of mention of business needs
interview_rounds.thoughts: I'm overall a very soft Yes on the portfolio presentation, mostly because the candidate's work is pretty solid, and their experience is quite relevant. However, I don't think they did amazingly well with their storytelling.
Detailed interview notes with answers on projects, goals, feedback, disagreements. Well/poorly sections left empty.

Background: Postmates (1.5y, designer/employee #2), Facebook (4y, Art director + designer), Contract for Pinterest/ClassDojo/SF Moma, Brex (creative director). Well/poorly sections left empty for both presentation and first round.

name: Stav Tobin
debrief_notes: Principles: No red flags, and no super green flags. Candidate seems solid overall. Portfolio: No red flags in this interview, but also nothing that particularly stood out and made me particularly excited about what this C could bring to the team.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Solid understanding of users and what their needs/problems are
- Interesting way to explore solutions using flow charts
- Overall appear mature in their approach to process and design. Willing to go beyond the existing system when possible
- Craft seems solid
- Pretty honest and candid
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- We didn't see a lot of explorations in the first project.
- I didn't get a clear understanding of how they selected the best solution(s) and how they iterated on them.
- Don't seem to have thought very deeply about the problem for the business. When asked about what success looked like, answer was very surface level.
- Collaboration with larger design team at Klarna didn't sound super deep

interview_rounds.thoughts: I'm a very soft 2. Didn't get super crisp signal, so I can be convinced the other way after seeing the portfolio. Detailed interview notes covering hardest project, ambition, stakeholder management, feedback, goals, project failures, tradeoffs, and techniques.

name: Stephanie Park
debrief_notes: Principles: Candidate is too fresh to be a bar raiser on the team, and I don't feel confident we have the right setup for them to quickly ramp up and be successful in an autonomous way. Portfolio: Promising candidate, who seems smart and confident in their process – but who is way too early in their journey for our roles and the level of autonomy we expect from our designers. They'd be a good bet if we were hiring a mid-level IC4.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- good intentionality in the work
- work is solid and addresses the problem statement + user needs
- leveraging the design system well and work is overall reasonably polished (though not groundbreaking)
- experience working on fairly complex products (futures)
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Projects were pretty small in scope. For example, the first of the 2 projects was a week long effort.
- First project wasn't really impact-oriented
- attention to detail: at the end of the flow for the first project, after celebratory moment, the demo made the user land in a place where they wouldn't even see their 40 USDC
- Very limited collaboration with other designers
interview_rounds.thoughts: Promising candidate, who seem smart and confident in their process - but who is way too early in their journey for our roles and the level of autonomy we expect from our designers, and I'm not confident they'll raise the bar.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Cares about craft and growing it, and is interested in what she heard from John B about our culture at Plaid
- Understands engineering constraints and is adaptable
- User centric work (although few mentions of research)
- Growing project scoping and prioritization sense
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Still growing their craft and admires senior ICs who can push the limits of the design system
- Though they work on advanced features, they don't seem to have dealt with much ambiguity yet
- Still learning to balance business impact, eng constraints and user needs
- Lack of ownership and ambition - couldn't share explicit mistakes they've made and own them
interview_rounds.thoughts: Candidate is too fresh to be a bar raiser on the team, and I don't feel confident we have the right setup for them to quickly ramp up and be successful in an autonomous way.

name: Stephen R. Fox
debrief_notes: Portfolio: Huge bummer of a portfolio review – the candidate chose to only show their work for 15 min, and didn't go in any depth. Disappointing given the experience they have and the type of work they've done. Based solely on this portfolio review, I'm a strong no. 1:1: Based on this interview alone, I'd be a soft 3. This C is overall aligned with our company values, though I'm not feeling very strongly about their capacity to self-drive their impact.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Good context setting, incl on expectations around Content Design at Twitter
- Good experience on consumer-facing experiences at Twitter, working on Onboarding, Settings, etc.
- As sole content designer for a while at Twitter, got a chance to work horizontally across the product, marketing, comms, etc.
- Clear speaker
- Good initiative - built a style guideline as a bridge between Brand guidelines and reality of the product.
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Very questionable use of time > long intros (15 min) + 15 min of actual work samples, then jumped to Q&A. Big red flag for 2 reasons: very little work shown, and positioned themselves more as a talker than a doer
- Didn't really show their system-level thinking, guideline establishing, etc
- Very unclear how they prioritize their work.
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Ex-Google (laid off), Ex-Twitter (5y)

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Great experience on consumer-facing work - working with compliance, internationalization, complex flows, etc, and horizontally from product to marketing and support
- "strong opinions, loosely held" attitude
- Fairly open with their growth areas and mistakes - though it required a little bit of prompting
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Their notion of impact was anchored in what the press had to say about a product they launched, not about the impact it had on the business or the users.
- I picked up a little bit of ego, especially around their work at Twitter.

interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Sr Director/ECD at Pinterest (50 person team, grew from 80 to 110 people, 500M to 600M MAUs, won first Webby Awards), Current (12-person executive team, led brand repositioning and first TV campaigns), Apple (iPhone 6 and Apple Watch launch), Google (first advertising campaign, Chrome launch "Dear Sophie" campaign), Nike (LeBron James campaigns). Creative leadership approach: never clears out existing teams, creates bigger creative vision, redesigned brief templates. Pinterest Predicts: doubled revenue, 1600% awareness increase.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Really great storyteller - managed to jump in after 10min of struggling with his internet and deliver in a very clear, engaging way
- Incredible track record of successful work and campaigns. The work for Chrome or Apple standing out.
- Brought the Pinterest Predicts campaign from B2B to B2B & B2C in a very successful way
- Visionary - sets the vision and enables the team to create with that sense of direction
- Almost never said "I" when talking about the work - consistently said "we" or "our team"
interview_rounds.did_not_well: - I would have loved a bit more behind-the-scenes depth to understand the direct impact Steve had on the work
interview_rounds.thoughts: Feeling really, really good about Steve. His portfolio of work speaks for itself, but he delivered it with great storytelling and communication skills. Steve brings vision, challenges the status quo, but seems pragmatic, empowering, kind and easy to work with. Steve doesn't have a ton of B2B experience - though his experience is so broad that I'm not nervous about him tackling something new.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Extensive Experience: Over 25 years across industries (corporate america, fintech) with expertise in program management, working with major companies like GE Capital and Kimberly Clark, and startups like Tally
- Adaptability & Resilience: Thrives in fast-paced, high-pressure (and chaotic) environments.
- Process Optimization: Proactively identifies and streamlines processes, as shown in her work at Plaid where she simplified an inefficient asset review system
- Balanced Leadership: Skilled at supporting visionary creatives by managing essential details, enabling big ideas to come to life effectively. Experienced in leading teams of 20+
- Passion for Innovation: Drawn to environments like Plaid that combine ambitious vision with top-tier talent
interview_rounds.thoughts: At Plaid: came in in May and immediately had to jump into Effects, picked things up quickly, can thrive in chaos. Project that didn't go well: thought her role was to project manage only the creative process, should have clarified who does what with other PMs.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Funny, engaging speaker
- Experience in tech and Saas (Patreon, Microsoft, Front, Descript)
- Experience creating writing guidelines
- Design decisions were well articulated and final work was strong
- Solid work across different channels, campaigns, web, etc - always aligned with the writing guidelines.
- Style and tone were well adjusted depending on the use case/audience
interview_rounds.thoughts: Background: Studied engineering, travels/hikes the world.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Long tenure in advertising agencies
- 8 years at LinkedIn climbing up to ECD
- Very craft oriented. Cares deeply about the work and quality of it. "Hard on the work, easy on the people"
- Had 2 creative strategists on her team, helping connect the briefs
- Tavia's strength is in finding the central, powerful idea in creatives' work, and is great at facilitating processes for selling and moving work through a large company
- Looking for depth and alignment between product and brand in her work
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- In-house experience limited to 1 large tech company (for a long time though). Hasn't operated in a startup-like environment like Plaid
- Current POV on AI may not be a great fit - seems to think it's only good for comp, not for producing any real work.
- Talked a lot about big campaigns, not a lot about performance marketing.

interview_rounds.thoughts: Well/poorly sections left empty.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Solid breadth of experience, leading design for different brands like Nike, Twitter, or Cash App
- Very relevant experience in Fintech at Cashapp, Chime and Wealthsimple. Understands some of the dynamics and the importance of trust.
- Creative mind, with a particular strong photography/video muscle (used to be a photographer for Nike).
- Skilled at creating an overarching vision for brands and curating elements to enhance that vision.
- Has led big teams (90+ people at Twitter) and teams of our size (20-ish at Chime).
- May not be as strong with copywriting (though that's just a guess)
interview_rounds.thoughts: Thierry brings a unique style and perspective combined with relevant experience that could bring us into interesting new directions. Let's see the work! Background: VP ECD at Wealthsimple (2y), Group CD at Cashapp (2y), Head of Creative at Chime (1.5y, 25+ in-house studio), Global CD at Twitter (2y, 90+ people), Nike (2y, Director), Photographer.
Well/poorly sections left empty.

name: Wesley Haines
debrief_notes: Wes would be a great candidate if we were looking at a high D5/early D6 role. But his profile is too similar to one we already have on the team and I'm not confident he could succeed in the role we're hiring for.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Great mix of experiences, having worked on internal tools at Spotify, monetization and systems at Duolingo, new apps at Nike, Google, Capital One, etc.
- Good technical background - showed some work on Spotify Config/dev tools for experimentation + wysiwyg editor + QA on different devices, has done some coding
- Working on monetization at Duolingo, and approaching that work in a thoughtful and pragmatic way, balancing business needs and user needs
- Very polished portfolio. Making the work shine well.
- Seem very motivated by the role
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Seem very fresh on the mentorship and leadership side. More of a high D5/early D6.
- Not as strong at execution as I was hoping they'd be. Rely a lot on existing design systems, have tried to build one at Duolingo but realized they weren't really good at it. Not a huge prototyping muscle either
interview_rounds.thoughts: I think Wes would be a great candidate if we were looking at a high D5/early D6 role. But his profile is too similar to one we already have on the team and I'm not confident he could succeed in the role we're hiring for.

Feedback shared: Your portfolio looks really great. Good breadth of experience, technical background, clearly motivated. For principal roles, we often look for the captain who can really level up everyone around them, on top of scoring. You have the track record for scoring, but not yet clear how you level up people around you. Suggested looking for Lead/Staff roles.

interview_rounds.did_well:
- Experience figuring out a workflow for video production at a SaaS company with few resources and hungry stakeholders. Managed to ramp up production to up to 4 videos a month.
- Supported entire creative team (12) across all campaigns
- Created a clear process for intake, prioritization, reviewing, execution
- Has a clear approach to get engagement from XFN or creatives: leaning in, assuming it's not working perfectly for them, and bringing curiosity and openness to updating the system if needed.
- Built systems to ensure stakeholders from various departments were equipped to contribute effectively to video projects and guided them to produce on-brand video content independently
interview_rounds.did_not_well:
- Communication wasn't the most engaging
- No signal on culture
- Why did she leave Sentry in Nov?
interview_rounds.thoughts: I'm a soft 3. Yana seems like a problem solver, seems organized, scrappy, and adapts to what the team needs to be successful. Her presentation skills aren't very strong and we didn't get much signal outside of this one case study. Also puzzled by the fact she left Sentry in November without anything lined up.
